title: Host Your Quarto Website on GitHub
text: This tutorial assumes you already have a rendered Quarto website on your local machine and want to host it publicly with GitHub Pages.

title: Host Your Quarto Website on GitHub
text:
Step 1: Get Git and GCM
Git is a free, open source, distributed version control system. In practice, it helps you track changes, back up your work on a remote service like GitHub, and collaborate with others.
First, check whether Git is already installed.

On Windows, look for Git Bash in the Start menu.
On macOS or Linux, run git --version in the terminal.

If a version number appears, Git is installed.
Even if Git is already installed, it is still worth installing Git Credential Manager (GCM) to make GitHub authentication much smoother.

Windows
Go to https://git-scm.com and download Git for Windows. This bundle includes Git, Git Bash, and Git Credential Manager.


macOS
The easiest route is Homebrew.
Check whether Homebrew is installed:
brew --version
If it is not installed, visit https://brew.sh, copy the installation command from the homepage, and run it in your terminal. It will look similar to this:
/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"
Follow the prompts in the terminal. You may need to enter your computer password or accept terms. When typing a password in the terminal, the cursor usually will not move, but your input is still being received.
Then install Git:
brew install git
If you get an error like zsh: command not found: brew, add Homebrew to your PATH and try again:
export PATH="/opt/homebrew/bin:$PATH" >> ~/.zshrc
Next, install Git Credential Manager:
brew install git-credential-manager

title: Host Your Quarto Website on GitHub
text:
Step 2: Introduce Yourself to Git
Git records an author name and email with each commit, so set that up once globally:
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your_email@example.com"
You can confirm your settings with:
git config --global --list
It is also helpful to ensure new repositories use main as the default branch name:
git config --global init.defaultBranch main

title: Host Your Quarto Website on GitHub
section: Step 3: Turn Git On for Your Website
text:
Step 3: Turn Git On for Your Website
Navigate to your website directory in the terminal, then initialize Git:
cd path/to/quarto_website
git init
This creates a hidden .git folder in your project.
If you want to view hidden files:

On macOS Finder, press Cmd+Shift+..
On Windows, search for “show hidden files” from the taskbar.
In RStudio, use the Files pane gear icon and enable Show Hidden Files.
In VS Code, update the files.exclude setting.

title: Host Your Quarto Website on GitHub
text:
Step 4: Save Your Website
Git starts tracking the directory after git init, but your project history begins only when you make commits.
A commit is a snapshot of your project. The usual flow is:

Stage files with git add.
Create the snapshot with git commit.

To stage all changed files and commit them:
git add .
git commit -m "Initial website commit"
You can also do this through RStudio or VS Code if you prefer a graphical interface.

title: Host Your Quarto Website on GitHub
text:
Step 5: Create a GitHub Account
Go to https://github.com and create a free account.
Choose a username that is short, professional, lowercase, and likely to age well.

title: Host Your Quarto Website on GitHub
text:
Step 6: Create a GitHub Repository
From GitHub:

Open the Repositories tab.
Click the green New button.

If you name the repository your_username.github.io, your site URL can be:
https://your_username.github.io
If you name it something else like quarto_website, the URL will usually be:
https://your_username.github.io/quarto_website
After creating the repository, GitHub will show a short set of commands under the heading for pushing an existing repository from the command line.

title: Host Your Quarto Website on GitHub
section: Step 7: Connect Your Local Directory to GitHub
text:
Step 7: Connect Your Local Directory to GitHub
Run the GitHub-provided commands in your local project directory:
git remote add origin https://github.com/username/repo-name.git
git branch -M main
git push -u origin main
What these commands do:

git remote add origin ... connects your local repository to GitHub.
git branch -M main renames the branch to main.
git push -u origin main uploads your local commits.

After pushing, refresh the GitHub repository page and you should see your website files there.

title: Host Your Quarto Website on GitHub
section: Step 8: Tell GitHub Your Repo Is a Website
text:
Step 8: Tell GitHub Your Repo Is a Website
In your GitHub repository:

Click Settings.
In the left menu, click Pages.
Under Branch, set:


Branch: main
Folder: docs

Give GitHub a minute or two to deploy the site. Once deployment finishes, GitHub will indicate that the site is live.

title: Host Your Quarto Website on GitHub
text:
Step 9: Confirm Your Public Website
Open your site URL in a browser or use the Visit site button from the GitHub Pages settings screen.

title: Host Your Quarto Website on GitHub
text:
Workflow to Update Your Website
As you continue working on the site, a practical workflow looks like this:

Edit files regularly, especially .qmd files and images.
Render locally often to preview your changes.
Commit your work periodically.
Push to GitHub less frequently, usually when you want to publish updates.

One common mistake is editing a .qmd file, committing it, and pushing it without rendering the website first. In that case, the source file changed but the generated HTML in docs/ did not, so the live website will not reflect your update.
Render locally before you commit and push so the published site matches your latest edits.

Host Your Quarto Website on GitHub


A tutorial for pushing a local Git repo to GitHub and deploying the website with GitHub Pages
